facts: In a representative democracy, citizens elect representatives to make laws on their behalf instead of voting on every issue directly.
The 15th Amendment (1870) gave African American men the right to vote, but women of all races were not guaranteed suffrage until the 19th Amendment (1920).
The Electoral College decides the presidency: a candidate needs at least 270 electoral votes out of 538 to win, even if they get fewer popular votes than another candidate.
A primary election narrows down the field of candidates within a political party, and the winner becomes the party's nominee for the general election.
The 26th Amendment (1971) lowered the voting age to 18, so that young people who could be drafted to fight in wars could also vote.
misconceptions: The candidate who wins the most popular votes nationwide always wins the presidency.
The 15th Amendment gave women the right to vote.
